title: Sampling frame and representativity
text: Why household survey frames must evolve with population change.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
text:
How to read this annex



Focus


Survey coverage and representativity




Scope


Methodological interpretation, not new estimates




Use


Read with Measurement and statistical quality




Boundary


Does not audit or replace official statistics

title: Sampling frame and representativity
text:
Why the frame matters
A sampling frame is the operational map from which a household survey draws its sample. In plain language, it defines the population coverage, organizes the units that can be selected, supports known probabilities of selection, and shapes what domains can be estimated with useful precision.
This is why the sampling frame is not a backstage detail. It affects the relationship between the survey sample and the population it is meant to represent. Good formulas and careful weighting still need a frame that reflects where households are and how the population is distributed.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
text:
Four things a frame does


Coverage
Defines who can enter the survey operation and which households are reachable through the design.


Selection
Organizes the stages and units used to select households under a probability sample.


Domains
Conditions which national, urban, rural, territorial or labor domains can be interpreted responsibly.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
section: ENEMDU and the challenge of time
text:
ENEMDU and the challenge of time
INEC’s sampling-design documentation explains that the ENEMDU sample frame is based on the final results and cartography of Ecuador’s 2010 Population and Housing Census, with partial cartographic updates and later adjustments to improve the construction of primary sampling units. The same documentation notes that population growth, decline and housing changes can alter how balanced those units are over time.
That statement should be read carefully. It does not mean that ENEMDU estimates are invalid. It means that a household survey frame is a living statistical infrastructure. Demographic change, migration, urban expansion and territorial change make documentation and modernization important for interpreting poverty, employment and territorial evidence.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
section: The frame is part of survey quality
text:
The frame is part of survey quality
The United Nations guide on household survey samples treats sampling frames and master samples as central pieces of household survey design. It links frame quality with probability sampling, domains, sample design documentation, weights and sampling errors.
The practical lesson is simple: household surveys are strongest when the frame, sample design, weighting, fieldwork and public documentation are read together. A static result table cannot carry that full context by itself.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
section: Why this matters for poverty and labor
text:
Why this matters for poverty and labor
Income poverty, deprivation, territorial poverty and labor-market indicators all depend on who is represented by the survey design. If population change concentrates in areas or groups that are harder to reach, the frame and its updates become part of the interpretation.
For poverty, the concern is not only the national rate. It is also whether urban, rural, provincial or subgroup readings are supported by the design. For labor, the concern extends to employment quality, subemployment, informality and people outside the labor force, because these groups can be unevenly distributed across geography and household types.
This monitor therefore treats domain reliability as part of the public story. More detailed disaggregation is useful only when the sampling frame, sample size and survey design support that level of reading.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
section: What this monitor can and cannot claim
text:
What this monitor can and cannot claim
This monitor does not audit INEC, does not replace official statistics, and does not correct the official sampling frame. It is a downstream analytical communication layer built from public information.
What it can do is make the role of survey design visible. Public microdata analysis should be read together with coverage, domain and representativity limits, especially when moving from national indicators to territorial or labor subgroup profiles.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
text:
A sober modernization agenda
Modernization is best framed as statistical maintenance, not as accusation. A prudent agenda would update household survey frames with more recent census and cartographic information, document the timing and scope of updates, evaluate territorial and labor domains, preserve comparable time series where possible, and publish clear metadata about changes.
That kind of transparency helps both official producers and downstream users. It lets readers distinguish real social change from design changes, sampling uncertainty or domain limits.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
text:
Reading note
This annex is methodological. It does not calculate new indicators, open microdata, assess field operations, or certify any estimate. It explains why poverty and labor interpretation should include the frame, the survey design and the domain of estimation.

title: Sampling frame and representativity
text:
Sources consulted
The page uses public methodological sources and cites them as context for interpretation:


INEC, Diseño Muestral ENEMDU, public sampling-design documentation used here as the December 2024 frame reference, including the note that the frame is based on the 2010 census cartography and has partial updates.
INEC, ENEMDU Anual documentation page, current public documentation hub for annual ENEMDU releases.
INEC, Metodología ENEMDU, official methodological documentation for the survey operation.
United Nations Statistics Division, Designing Household Survey Samples: Practical Guidelines, Studies in Methods Series F No. 98.
World Bank, Living Standards Measurement Study, household survey program focused on strengthening survey quality, relevance and timeliness.

title: Territorial view of income poverty
text: Provincial patterns from annual ENEMDU 2025

title: Territorial view of income poverty
text:
What this view covers



Period


Annual ENEMDU 2025




Measure


Income poverty




Geography


24 provinces




Reading


Incidence and concentration

title: Territorial view of income poverty
text:
One map, two readings
The provincial map shows where poverty rates are highest. The executive table answers a different question: where the estimated number of people in poverty is largest.
That distinction matters. Esmeraldas has the highest poverty rate among the provinces highlighted in the incidence comparison, while Guayas has the largest estimated number of people in poverty: about 932,505 people, with a poverty rate of 20.0%.

title: Territorial view of income poverty
text:
Where rates are highest

Esmeraldas

Bolívar



28.2%


Manabí

These rates compare the intensity of income poverty across provinces. They should be read as analytical estimates, especially for smaller domains where sampling uncertainty can matter.

title: Territorial view of income poverty
text:
Where the burden is largest
The five provinces in the executive table account for about 2,257,759 estimated people in poverty. This ranking is based on the estimated number of people in poverty, not only on the poverty rate.
This helps separate territorial incidence from population concentration: a province can have a lower rate and still contain many more people in poverty because its population is much larger.

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
text: Annual 2025 executive profile of income poverty, basic-needs deprivation and multidimensional poverty.

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
text:
Scope



Period


2025 annual ENEMDU




Source


Public ENEMDU annual 2025 microdata




Coverage


National, urban and rural




Engine


enemduR




Output


Curated aggregate RDS




Method


Survey-weighted analytical estimates

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
section: Three poverty lenses, one reading problem
text:
Three poverty lenses, one reading problem
The national income-poverty rate is 22.0%, while NBI reaches 30.0% and TPM reaches 37.0%. These are not competing versions of the same statistic. Income poverty identifies insufficient current resources, NBI captures accumulated structural deficits, and TPM captures overlapping weighted deprivations.
The curated output represents about 18,775,415 people nationally for the income-poverty denominator and stores only aggregate fields used by this page.

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
text:
Rural disadvantage is systematic
Rural areas show the highest burden in 6 of 6 indicators displayed. The largest urban-rural gap appears in Multidimensional poverty / TPM, which indicates that territorial inequality is not confined to monetary poverty.

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
text:
Monetary and structural poverty diverge
The national NBI rate is 30.0%, above the national income-poverty rate of 22.0%. Reading both together separates insufficient current income from accumulated deficits in housing, services, schooling and household dependency conditions.

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
text:
TPM captures overlapping deprivation
TPM reaches 37.0% nationally and rural TPM is about 2.7x the urban rate. TPEM reaches 14.4% nationally, showing the severe tail of overlapping deprivations.

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
text:
Benchmark status matters
Income poverty is benchmarked against public annual tabulations and shows close-match status. NBI is not directly benchmarked against public annual tabulations in this build. TPM/TPEM are calculated with enemduR and their public IPM benchmark comparison is marked Benchmark requires review, so this page treats them as analytical estimates with an explicit reproducibility flag.

title: Deprivation and multidimensional poverty
text:
Method note
The indicators are based on public ENEMDU annual 2025 microdata and built with enemduR. Estimates are survey-weighted. Benchmark comparison is reported only where a public annual reference is available. The comparison documents analytical reproducibility and does not imply official institutional validation.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Why it matters




The problem is not the lack of data. The problem is the missing reading layer.

Poverty data in Ecuador already exist across official bulletins, microdata, methodological notes, poverty lines, labor reports, monthly releases, quarterly files, and annual databases. This monitor turns that scattered public information into a coherent, reproducible, and decision-oriented reading.




The Ecuador Poverty Monitor connects income, deprivation, territory, labor conditions, and statistical quality into one static Quarto product.
It does not replace official statistics. It helps explain how to read them.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
The Ecuador Poverty Monitor connects income, deprivation, territory, labor conditions, and statistical quality into one static Quarto product.
It does not replace official statistics. It helps explain how to read them.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
What is fragmented?
The issue is not a shortage of information. The issue is that each release answers only part of the question.
Tables can inform, but poverty analysis needs context: what was measured, what period supports the estimate, which territorial domains are valid, and how precise the result is.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
What the monitor connects
Poverty can be read through income, basic needs, simultaneous deprivation, territory, and work. Each lens illuminates a different part of the problem.
When those pieces are read separately, the picture remains incomplete. When they are connected, the country becomes easier to understand.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Source, engine, product
ENEMDU is Ecuador’s National Survey of Employment, Unemployment, and Underemployment. It is one of the country’s key statistical operations for analyzing work, labor conditions, income, poverty, and household characteristics.


1. Official source
The base information comes from ENEMDU and its methodological documentation.


2. Analytical engine
enemduR organizes reading, validation, estimation, indicators, and analytical outputs.


3. Narrative product
This site turns results into a visual and explanatory story.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Three speeds, different questions
This product does not treat all periods as if they supported the same type of reading. The monitor is organized into monthly pulse, quarterly city view, and annual structural analysis.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Golden rule
Never show a territorial disaggregation that does not match the representativity domain of the period being used. Methodological clarity is part of the product, not a secondary note.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
A compact product contract
This monitor adds an integrated reading of poverty, a reproducible architecture based on enemduR, a stronger visual communication layer, a clear distinction between pulse and structure, and a technical discussion of representativity.

title: Why build a poverty monitor?
text:
Not a parallel authority
The monitor does not replace INEC or produce official statistics. Its goal is to transform complex public information into an ordered, reproducible, visual, and useful reading of poverty in Ecuador.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Poverty intelligence for Ecuador



Every time a poverty rate is published, the country can appear to fit inside one percentage point. Ecuador does not fit inside one number.
This monitor organizes income poverty, deprivation, territory, labor conditions, and statistical quality into one reproducible editorial product.

Start with the purpose View income poverty





The monitor separates short-term signals from structural poverty analysis, so comparisons stay meaningful.



Monthly

Pulse

National, urban/rural, and labor-market context.




Quarterly

Cities

Quito, Guayaquil, Cuenca, Machala, and Ambato when the domain supports it.




Annual

Structure

Poverty, income distribution, NBI, multidimensional poverty, and provincial territory.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Monthly pulse
Latest available month
Short-term context for national and urban/rural indicators, especially labor-market conditions.
This layer helps read the present without confusing volatility with structure.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Quarterly view
Five main cities
Intermediate monitoring for Quito, Guayaquil, Cuenca, Machala, and Ambato when the sample design supports it.
This layer surfaces urban signals more frequently than the annual analysis.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Annual core
Full structure
Income poverty, quintiles, NBI, multidimensional poverty, provinces, cities, and territorial analysis.
This is the narrative foundation of the monitor.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
2025 annual income poverty snapshot
In 2025, Ecuador’s annual income poverty rate reached 21.95%. The annual reading shows a strong territorial contrast: 16.51% in urban areas and 33.63% in rural areas. Extreme poverty reached 6.99% nationally, with 3.69% in urban areas and 14.07% in rural areas.

These estimates are computed from public ENEMDU microdata and compared against public annual INEC tabulations for analytical reproducibility. This is not an official institutional validation.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Why this monitor exists
Public poverty data in Ecuador are valuable, but the reading is often spread across bulletins, microdata, technical notes, methodological reports, and isolated tables.
The problem is not the absence of data. The problem is that the story connecting the data is often fragmented.
The Ecuador Poverty Monitor is designed as a reproducible communication layer. It does not replace official statistics or create a parallel statistical authority. It helps readers understand what is measured, how it is measured, what can be compared, and where each estimate has limits.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
The core idea
A useful poverty monitor should not only ask how many people are poor.


Condition
Income, deprivation, and the relative position of households.


Place
Territory, cities, rurality, and valid domains of estimation.


Measurement
Precision, representativity, and comparability across periods.

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text: How to read the site

title: Poverty is not a single number.
text:
Product status

This site is being built in public as a static Quarto portfolio product.
The 2025 annual income-poverty page is published with benchmark comparison against public annual INEC tabulations. The deprivation and multidimensional poverty page is published with income poverty, NBI, extreme NBI, TPM, and TPEM indicators. The territorial view of income poverty adds a provincial map and an executive table separating poverty incidence from population concentration. The labor-market page adds a public reading of employment conditions and poverty using curated aggregate outputs. The methodology and statistical quality page documents how benchmark comparisons, quality metadata, domains, and publication limits should be read.



Official microdata
Built around public ENEMDU source files.


Methodological contracts
Periods and domains are explicit before analysis.


Statistical quality
Representativity and precision guide what can be shown.


Reproducible output
Quarto consumes derived products, not raw microdata.



Why it matters -> Income poverty -> Deprivation and multidimensional poverty -> Territorial view of income poverty -> Labor market and poverty -> Measurement and statistical quality ->

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Capa ejecutiva en español



El sitio principal está escrito en inglés para funcionar como producto de portafolio internacional. Esta página resume la propuesta para lectores ecuatorianos y audiencias locales.
El objetivo es explicar pobreza, territorio, empleo y calidad estadística con una narrativa reproducible, sobria y útil.

Ver sitio principal en inglés Ver pobreza por ingresos





Organiza datos públicos de ENEMDU en una lectura clara, separando coyuntura, estructura y límites de representatividad.



Mensual

Pulso

Contexto nacional, urbano/rural y laboral reciente.




Trimestral

Ciudades

Señales urbanas cuando el dominio de estimación lo permite.




Anual

Estructura

Pobreza, privaciones, quintiles y territorio provincial.

text:

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Para qué sirve
El Ecuador Poverty Monitor no reemplaza estadísticas oficiales ni crea una autoridad paralela. Su función es convertir información pública compleja en una lectura ordenada, visual y reproducible.
Ayuda a responder qué se está midiendo, qué comparaciones son válidas y qué límites tiene cada estimación.

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Cápsula anual 2025
En 2025, la pobreza por ingresos alcanzó 21,95% a nivel nacional. La brecha territorial es marcada: 16,51% en el área urbana y 33,63% en el área rural. La pobreza extrema fue de 6,99% a nivel nacional, 3,69% en el área urbana y 14,07% en el área rural.

Estos resultados se calculan a partir de microdatos públicos de ENEMDU y se comparan con tabulados públicos anuales del INEC con fines de reproducibilidad analítica. No constituyen validación institucional oficial.

title: Entender la pobreza más allá de una sola cifra.
text:
Tres capas


Fuente
ENEMDU y documentación metodológica oficial.


Motor
Procesamiento reproducible mediante enemduR.


Producto
Narrativa editorial, gráficos e infografías futuras.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Income poverty




This page turns the annual ENEMDU poverty results into a compact reading of income, territory, and benchmark consistency.

In 2025, Ecuador’s annual income poverty rate reached 21.95%. The national figure is the starting point, but the substantive story is territorial: poverty reached 16.51% in urban areas and 33.63% in rural areas.




This page summarizes Ecuador’s 2025 annual income poverty profile, focusing on the national rate, the urban-rural gap, extreme poverty, and benchmark consistency.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text: This page summarizes Ecuador’s 2025 annual income poverty profile, focusing on the national rate, the urban-rural gap, extreme poverty, and benchmark consistency.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
How to read this page







Field
Contract




Unit of analysis
People represented through ENEMDU survey weights


Measure
Share of people in households below the income poverty or extreme income poverty line


Scale
Estimates are shown as percentages


Source
Public ENEMDU annual microdata


Benchmark
Public annual INEC tabulations for 2025


Validation type
Reproducibility comparison against a public reference, not institutional validation

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Income poverty
The annual estimate shows that roughly one in five people lived in households whose per-capita income did not reach the income poverty line. The national rate was 21.95%.
The monitor treats this figure as an annual structural reading, not as a monthly pulse. That distinction matters because the annual ENEMDU file supports a broader territorial and thematic analysis than short-term releases.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
A deeper income threshold
Extreme income poverty reached 6.99% nationally in 2025. The territorial contrast is again sharp: 3.69% in urban areas and 14.07% in rural areas.
This layer separates income poverty from extreme income poverty because the two indicators answer different questions. Poverty identifies households below the income poverty line; extreme poverty identifies households below a more restrictive minimum threshold.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Urban-rural gap at a glance


Income poverty gap


Urban16.51%





Rural33.63%





Rural minus urban: 17.12 percentage points.
Rural rate: 2.04x the urban rate.


Extreme poverty gap


Urban3.69%





Rural14.07%





Rural minus urban: 10.38 percentage points.
Rural rate: 3.81x the urban rate.


Interpretive signal
The rural disadvantage is visible in both poverty thresholds, but it becomes proportionally sharper for extreme poverty.
This is why the monitor keeps income poverty and extreme income poverty separate instead of collapsing them into a single headline.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
What the gaps add
Income poverty is 17.12 percentage points higher in rural areas than in urban areas. In relative terms, the rural rate is about 2.04 times the urban rate.
Extreme poverty is 10.38 percentage points higher in rural areas. The rural extreme-poverty rate is about 3.81 times the urban rate, which means the territorial divide is even steeper at the deepest income threshold.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
section: Who is more exposed to income poverty?
text:
Who is more exposed to income poverty?
The annual profile shows that income poverty is not evenly distributed across population groups. Children and adolescents have the highest poverty rate in this first profile layer, and adult education shows a clear gradient: poverty falls as educational attainment rises.
The sex difference is much smaller than the age and education gradients, which keeps the interpretation focused on structural exposure rather than small headline differences.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Income poverty by age group
Children and adolescents show the highest poverty exposure in this first profile layer. The gradient softens across working ages and remains close to 15% among older adults.

0-1430.57%
15-2423.84%
25-4418.79%
45-6414.88%
65+15.07%

The child and adolescent rate is roughly twice the rate observed among people aged 45-64.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
section: Income poverty by adult education level
text:
Income poverty by adult education level
Income poverty declines sharply as educational attainment rises. The contrast between adults with no formal education or literacy center and adults with higher education is the steepest profile shown here.

No formal education or literacy center29.80%
Primary or basic education23.57%
Secondary or bachillerato15.46%
Higher education7.71%

The adult education profile points to a clear structural gradient, not a small compositional difference.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Income poverty by sex
The sex gap is narrower than the age and education gradients, so it should be read as a secondary contrast in this first profile layer.

Female22.62%
Male21.26%

The profile gap by sex is modest compared with the differences by age and adult education.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
section: Extreme poverty follows the same vulnerability pattern
text:
Extreme poverty follows the same vulnerability pattern
Selected profile estimates point in the same direction: extreme poverty reaches 10.36% for children and adolescents, 11.23% among adults with no formal education or literacy center, and 2.11% among adults with higher education.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
Source and interpretation
These profiles are survey-weighted analytical estimates derived from annual ENEMDU microdata. They are not directly benchmarked against public annual tabulations, so they should be read as analytical profiles rather than benchmark-validated headline estimates.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
text:
How the evidence is grounded
The headline estimates are computed from public ENEMDU annual microdata and compared with public annual INEC tabulations. The repository documents the processing workflow and generated outputs for reproducibility. This comparison is not an official institutional validation.
The page shows aggregated results only. It does not expose survey files, household records, person records, local paths, or private data locations.

Close match means the monitor estimate differs from the public annual benchmark by no more than 0.5 percentage points. The benchmark comparison is a reproducibility check against a public annual reference table; it documents analytical reproducibility and does not imply official institutional validation.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
section: What this page does not claim
text:
What this page does not claim
This page does not estimate causality, explain every driver of poverty, or create a new official statistic. It presents a stable annual analytical reading based on aggregated results.
Distributional analysis will be added once quintile or income-distribution outputs are available. The next versions of the monitor should add uncertainty, representativity notes, and deeper territorial detail only where the survey design supports those domains.

title: Ecuador’s 2025 annual poverty profile
section: What comes after the first layer
text:
What comes after the first layer
The next income-poverty layer can add poverty-line context, precision indicators, historical comparisons, and distributional readings. Deprivation, multidimensional poverty, labor, and province-level analysis should remain separate layers until their own publication rules are complete.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text: How the monitor separates analytical estimates, benchmark comparison and public communication.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
The core distinction
A benchmark comparison for analytical reproducibility checks whether a result aligns with a public reference under a stated tolerance.
It is not official institutional validation, certification, endorsement, or approval.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
What this monitor is
The monitor is a downstream analytical and communication layer built from public ENEMDU source files, explicit methodological contracts, reproducible processing with enemduR, curated aggregate outputs, and static Quarto pages.
It organizes results so readers can see what was estimated, for which period and domain, with which quality metadata, and against which public reference when a suitable benchmark is available.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
What this monitor is not
It is not an official statistical release, a substitute for INEC publications, or an independent statistical authority. It does not claim institutional validation. It also does not treat every analytical estimate as equally benchmarked or equally suitable for territorial disaggregation.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
Four publication layers


1. Source microdata
Public ENEMDU files are processed locally. Microdata remain outside the public site and are not copied into versioned publication outputs.


2. Annual analytical outputs
Local derived products hold the analytical results required by the pipeline. They are separate from the website and are not intended as direct public interfaces.


3. Curated site outputs
Versionable publication files contain only the aggregate fields needed by Quarto. They exclude microdata, raw identifiers, private paths, and dynamic timestamps.


4. Quarto pages
Static pages consume curated outputs and add interpretation, benchmark labels, quality notes, and explicit limits without recalculating the underlying indicators.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
Three-speed architecture


Monthly
The monthly layer is a short-term pulse for national and urban-rural context, particularly labor-market conditions. It should not be read as a substitute for annual structural poverty analysis.


Quarterly
The quarterly layer supports intermediate urban monitoring, including the five main cities only when the survey design and configured domain contract support those estimates.


Annual
The annual layer is the structural core for income poverty, deprivation, multidimensional poverty, distribution, and broader territorial analysis. The current published poverty profiles use annual 2025 outputs.


Period and domain are linked: a territorial level is shown only when it is supported by the survey design for that period.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
Benchmark status
benchmark_status records the result of a comparison with an identified public reference. It is a reproducibility field, not an institutional seal.









Benchmark status
Meaning
Public interpretation
Current use in the site




Close match
The analytical estimate falls within the stated comparison tolerance for the available public reference.
The monitor closely reproduces that public benchmark under the documented analytical contract.
Income poverty and extreme income poverty.


Not directly benchmarked
No equivalent public annual table is used for a direct indicator-level comparison in this build.
Read the result as a survey-weighted analytical estimate, not as a benchmark-matched headline.
NBI and extreme NBI.


Outside tolerance / requires review
A public reference is available, but the analytical comparison does not meet the current tolerance.
The estimate may be published with a visible review flag, but it must not be described as a close reproduction.
TPM and TPEM.


Reference missing or not matched
The expected comparison cannot be completed for the relevant row or reference.
Do not infer benchmark alignment; inspect the source and matching contract before making a claim.
Reserved for pipeline transparency when applicable.




For the current annual income-poverty comparison, “close match” means a difference of no more than 0.5 percentage points from the public annual reference. That tolerance belongs to this analytical reproducibility check and does not imply official institutional validation.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
Current indicator status








Indicator
What it measures
Current benchmark interpretation




Income poverty
Whether household per-capita income falls below the income-poverty line.
Close match against the public annual benchmark.


Extreme income poverty
Whether household per-capita income falls below the extreme-poverty line.
Close match against the public annual benchmark.


NBI
Structural deprivation measured through unmet basic needs.
Not directly benchmarked against a public annual table in this build.


Extreme NBI
More severe or accumulated unmet-basic-needs conditions under the implemented method.
Not directly benchmarked against a public annual table in this build.


TPM
Multidimensional poverty based on overlapping weighted deprivations and the implemented threshold.
Benchmark comparison outside tolerance; requires methodological review.


TPEM
Severe multidimensional poverty based on the implemented extreme threshold.
Benchmark comparison outside tolerance; requires methodological review.



These indicators answer different questions. Income poverty concerns current monetary resources. NBI captures structural deficits in basic needs. TPM and TPEM summarize overlapping weighted deprivations. They should be read together, not treated as interchangeable poverty rates.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
text:
Survey-weighted analytical estimates
The published indicators are survey-weighted analytical estimates. Survey weights translate sampled observations into estimates for the population represented by the valid survey domain.

weighted_n is the estimated population represented by the analytical denominator.
unweighted_n is the number of survey records contributing to that denominator.
quality_flag carries the available precision or representativity assessment from the analytical output.
benchmark_status describes benchmark comparison, which is separate from sample quality.

A large weighted_n does not by itself guarantee precision, and a benchmark close match does not replace review of sample size, domain validity, and quality flags.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
section: Read every estimate inside its contract
text:
Read every estimate inside its contract
The current poverty pages publish national, urban, and rural annual domains. Each estimate also belongs to a defined universe and analysis unit. Readers should not compare or combine rows without checking that period, universe, analysis unit, and domain are compatible.
More detailed geography is not automatically more informative. City, province, or other territorial estimates should appear only in the layers and periods where the survey design supports them. Unsupported disaggregation can create false precision even when the underlying calculation runs successfully.
For a deeper public note on survey coverage, sampling frames and population change, read the Sampling frame and representativity annex.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
section: How to read a quality flag
text:
How to read a quality flag
A quality flag is a decision aid, not decoration. If the field is available, read its value before publishing or interpreting a subgroup estimate. A restrictive or review-oriented flag should lead to qualification, suppression, or further methodological inspection according to the producing method. If the field is unavailable, the absence of a warning must not be interpreted as evidence of high precision.

title: Methodology and statistical quality
Methodology and statistical quality
text:
Current limitations

This monitor is not an official statistical release and does not carry institutional validation.
NBI and extreme NBI do not have a direct public annual benchmark inside the current build.
TPM and TPEM require methodological review before they can be described as close reproductions of the public reference.
The current annual pages focus on national, urban, and rural domains; finer disaggregation requires a separate representativity contract.
Future territorial and labor layers require their own curated outputs, quality checks, and publication rules.
Static publication improves auditability, but it does not remove the need to review methods when source files, survey design, or official references change.

title: Labor market and poverty
text: Employment conditions and household vulnerability in annual ENEMDU 2025

title: Labor market and poverty
text:
What this view covers



Period


Annual ENEMDU 2025




Source


Curated aggregate site output




Coverage


National, urban and rural




Reading


Employment, job quality and poverty profiles

title: Labor market and poverty
section: Why having work does not always mean being out of poverty
text:
Why having work does not always mean being out of poverty
Poverty is measured through household per-capita income, not only through whether a person has a job. That distinction is crucial: a person can be employed and still live in a household whose total income is below the income-poverty line.
The national profile shows that poverty among employed people is 15.9%, while poverty among unemployed people is 25.8% and poverty among people outside the labor force is 21.9%. The estimated number of people in poverty is largest among employed people, at about 1,317,829.

title: Labor market and poverty
section: Who is attached to the labor market?
text:
Who is attached to the labor market?


Labor force participation



64.3%


Employment among people 15+



62.0%


Outside the labor force



35.7%


Unemployment



3.6%


The first reading separates labor-force attachment from unemployment. Many people are outside the labor force, while unemployment is measured only among people in the labor force.

title: Labor market and poverty
text:
Work status is not enough


Adequate/full employment



38.5%


Subemployment



20.1%


Among employed people, adequate/full employment reaches 38.5% and subemployment reaches 20.1%. This is why the labor layer looks beyond the employed/unemployed split: job quality changes how employment should be read alongside household income poverty.

The table compares broad labor conditions, not provinces or cities. The count column shows where the largest number of people in poverty is concentrated, while the poverty-rate columns show exposure within each labor condition.

title: Labor market and poverty
section: Poverty is not only a story about unemployment
text:
Poverty is not only a story about unemployment


Employed



15.9%


Unemployed



25.8%


Outside the labor force



21.9%


Unemployment is associated with a high poverty rate, but it accounts for a smaller estimated number of people in poverty than employment. The national estimate shows about 1,039,215 people in poverty outside the labor force, another reminder that household vulnerability extends beyond the labor-force headline.

title: Labor market and poverty
text:
Reading notes
These estimates are analytical and descriptive. They are based on public ENEMDU annual 2025 microdata and are published here only through a curated aggregate output. They are not an official INEC validation, do not make causal claims, and should be read together with Measurement and statistical quality.
